paragraphs.body: [The scene starts off with me and mama at the kitchen table. It is a hot summer day, and I am much too short for my feet to touch the floor. The kitchen window is ajar, displaying the alleyway in the back.]
[ My legs aimlessly dangle off the shabby wooden chair, as momma peers intently into my eyes.]

Me: “Mama, why did you decide to have me?” [Mama looks at me as if I had said something gravely offensive.] Mama: “We wanted you, baby.” Me: “Wanted?” Mama: “You were planned. I wanted you, and knew what that would entail.” Me: “Ohkay!” [My foot swings against the chair, hitting the wood. Thud. Thud. Thud. Thud. My mom starts thinking intently, before continuing.] Mama: “Yes, even when I was a little girl I had a name picked out for you!” Me: “Woah, really?” [I smile. My mom really put all of her love, hopes, and dreams into my soul.] Mama: “When I was just around your age, I collected a book series called “Angelique’s Adventures. It’s about a French princess who fell in love and went on adventures.” Me: “Wow mama, that’s amazing...I wanna read books like that someday!” [Mama gets up, and checks the fridge. She turns back to me.] Mama: “Baby, can you go get milk, bread, and eggs from the supermarket for tomorrow's breakfast?” [I get handed 2 $10 bills.] Me: “Okay mama! Don’t we also need Mama: “That’s my baby!” [I go to fetch my keys, and open the door to head out.] Mama: “Wait- one more thing!” [I pause.] Mama: “Don’t forget the butter!”
[ I walk down the stairs, and head out onto the street.]

Step step step step, I walk down the street, feeling the hot air press against my freshly done do’. Step step step I hear the soft jingles of the keys in my pocket as I make my way down the block and past the corner I reach in my pocket and finger the 2 crumpled $10 bills mama gave me for milk and bread, eggs and butter if I could find some on sale, Rustle rustle rustle I clutch the bills tighter as I step step step down into the supermarket, cool air conditioning hits my face, I head for the bread. Step step step down the linoleum floors, into the bread asile. Fresh sweet bread wafts down into my nose, jingle jingle jingle of keys as I reach for the white bread and smell it. I tuck the bread under the crook of my arm and clappp clapp clappping of my flip flops hits the cold floor, I bound down to the dairy section. I see the perfect plastic jug of milk, I grab the butter, and taptaptap down the floors it as the liquid sloshsloshsloshes around, Eggs, I have to get the eggs or else Mama’ll send me back to pick them up, Where are the eggs again..I and stepstepstep to the eggs, and peer down at the Styrofoam cartons, wow they look perfect just stacked up like that-..,eggs are grabbed, now i have to pay... Stepstepstep on the cool floor back to the cashier... [The cashier rings up the items, and peers down at me.] Cashier: “Twelve forty-five, mami.”
[I slide the money across, pay for my items, and head out of the store.]
I’m going to share a quote from my favorite book in the world, “A Tree Grows in Brooklyn”, by Betty Smith.

“ A person who pulls himself up from a low environment via the bootstrap route has two choices. Having risen above his environment, he can forget it; or, he can rise above it and never forget it and keep compassion and understanding in his heart for those he has left behind him in the cruel upclimb.” - A Tree Grows in Brooklyn, Betty Smith This quote represents what it’s like to move forwards in life and still keep your compassion and empathy. It’s one of my favorite quotes at that, because no matter how far in life I’ll claw and climb my way up, I’ll never forget my humble roots, and the little girl that stood at the beginning of that journey. Moving on, whenever I meet indivusals starting on their own journeys, I will hold the same empathy in my heart. I will always remember being that little girl sitting on a blanket outstretched over the fire escape on a warm day, book in hand, glass of water angled carefully next to her.
